entry.title: Re: Facing issue while deploying a UI5 application
entry.content:
@Fabrice That is the updated mta file :

_schema-version: "3.2"
ID: ordersmanagement
version: 0.0.1
modules:
- name: ordersmanagement-destination-content
  type: com.sap.application.content
  requires:
  - name: ordersmanagement-destination-service
    parameters:
      content-target: true
  - name: ordersmanagement_html_repo_host
    parameters:
      service-key:
        name: ordersmanagement_html_repo_host-key
  - name: uaa_ordersmanagement
    parameters:
      service-key:
        name: uaa_ordersmanagement-key
  parameters:
    content:
      subaccount:
        destinations:
        - Name: ordersmanagement_ordersmanagement_html_repo_host
          ServiceInstanceName: ordersmanagement-html5-app-host-service
          ServiceKeyName: ordersmanagement_html_repo_host-key
          sap.cloud.service: ordersmanagement
        - Authentication: OAuth2UserTokenExchange
          Name: ordersmanagement_uaa_ordersmanagement
          ServiceInstanceName: ordersmanagement-xsuaa-service
          ServiceKeyName: uaa_ordersmanagement-key
          sap.cloud.service: ordersmanagement
        existing_destinations_policy: ignore
  build-parameters:
    no-source: true
- name: ordersmanagement-app-content
  type: com.sap.application.content
  path: .
  requires:
  - name: ordersmanagement_html_repo_host
    parameters:
      content-target: true
  build-parameters:
    build-result: resources
    requires:
    - artifacts:
      - ordersmgtnspworkflowuimodule.zip
      name: ordersmgtnspworkflowuimodule
      target-path: resources/
- name: ordersmgtnspworkflowuimodule
  type: html5
  path: workflow-ui-module
  build-parameters:
    build-result: dist
    builder: custom
    commands:
    - npm install
    - npm run build:cf
    supported-platforms: []
resources:

      version: 1.0.0

- name: uaa_ordersmanagement
  type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
  parameters:
    path: ./xs-security.json
    service: xsuaa
    service-name: ordersmanagement-xsuaa-service
    service-plan: application
parameters:
  deploy_mode: html5-repo

entry.content:
No, I have not - is that a prerequisite?

For my understanding, the reference date variable purpose is to be a dynamic "cut" your data based on a specific date. To get an understandig of: "How was the status at July 1st" (when you are in e.g. September)? 

And it still seems a bit odd to me, that I can see the attributes in the table, but not the chart?

Reason is, I want to display both a current and last year actual in the same chart, as to separate bars, and then put a variance on top.

entry.title: Re: Metadata download button not available in SAP Mobile...
entry.content:
@akhthaaralibadhusha This settings might direct linked to the multi user check in client settings option.

Please try the following:

  • Go to any of existing app in mobile services admin UI
  • Client Settings 
  • Scroll down >check Allow Upload of Pending Changes from Previous User (Enable Multiple User Mode):
  • Save the changes
  • Go to Settings > Security > do you now see Metadata Download option?
  • If so, you can go back to Client settings to uncheck multiuser mode option, Save. Metadata Download option should remain there

entry.title: Attendance Punching Machine database data reading
entry.content:
I have multiple attendance machines that store fingerprints in a SQL Server database.
I plan to connect SAP with SQL Server using a DBCON connection and develop an ABAP program to read and insert records into the TEVEN table or a Z-table.
Is this approach correct? Should I use BAPIs to update TEVEN or write directly? Any advice on job scheduling and duplicate record handling?

entry.title: Set up the SAP Cloud Connector: Integrating On‑Premise Systems with Cloud Services
entry.content:
Set up the SAP Cloud Connector: Integrating On‑Premise SAP Systems with Cloud Services Using the Cloud Connector

In today’s hybrid IT landscape, connecting on‑premise SAP systems with cloud services is a strategic necessity and a best practice ecomended by SAP. This blog explains how to establish secure connections between your internal SAP environment and cloud platform, such as the Business Technology Platform, Integration Suite, or SAP Build Apps, using the Cloud Connector. We cover the key connection protocols, compare three OData service deployment strategies, and outline the service creation process with relevant SAP transaction codes. Finally, we detail the steps to set up the Cloud Connector with proper certificate management and configuration paths, ensuring a secure and efficient integration.

Connection Protocols and Their Roles

Before configuring your integration, it is essential to understand the protocols that underpin communication between systems:

  • HTTP/HTTPS:
    These protocols form the basis for web-based communication. HTTPS, in particular, ensures that data transferred between cloud services and on‑premise systems is encrypted and secure—critical for protecting sensitive business information.
  • RFC (Remote Function Call):
    RFC enables communication between different SAP components. When integrated with the Cloud Connector, RFC calls are securely transmitted from cloud applications to your on‑premise SAP system, ensuring consistency and reliability in data and process exchanges.
  • OData (Open Data Protocol):
    OData is the standard protocol for exposing SAP Business Suite data as RESTful services. It supports CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete), making it ideal for modern applications that require flexible and real‑time data interaction across multiple devices.

Interface Connections and Deployment Strategies

Interface connections are the channels through which data flows between your on‑premise SAP systems and cloud platforms. When exposing SAP Business Suite data via OData services, you can choose among three primary deployment strategies:

  1. Embedded Deployment:
    With Embedded Deployment, available from SAP NetWeaver 7.4 onward, the SAP Gateway Foundation is integrated within each SAP Business Suite system. Each system runs its own SAP Gateway instance, meaning that you must configure the gateway individually for each backend system. This approach is ideal when data needs to be exposed independently for each system, although it does require managing multiple configurations.
  2. Central HUB Deployment:
    In this model, the SAP Gateway is installed on a dedicated front‑end server (the hub system) rather than being embedded in every backend system. A single SAP Gateway instance serves all SAP Business Suite systems, centralizing connectivity and enhancing security.
  3. OData Provisioning via Cloud-Based Deployment:
    This strategy moves the SAP Gateway frontend to the cloud. Here, the SAP Cloud Platform manages the gateway while OData services connect to on‑premise systems (typically SAP HANA) via the Cloud Connector. The Cloud Connector, acting as an on‑premise agent, initiates outbound connections, thereby eliminating the need to open inbound firewall ports. This not only enhances security but also leverages modern, centrally managed, browser-based development environments like the cloud version of SAP Web IDE.

Why Use the SAP Cloud Connector?

Even if your OData services appear to be accessible directly, employing the SAP Cloud Connector provides substantial benefits beyond basic connectivity:

Enhanced Security via Reverse Invoke
SAP Cloud Connector uses a secure, outbound "reverse invoke" approach. Instead of opening risky inbound firewall ports that could expose your internal network to threats, the connector initiates secure connections outward from your protected on-premise environment to the SAP Cloud. This significantly reduces vulnerabilities by preventing unauthorized external access.

Centralized Connection Management
By providing a centralized point of control, SAP Cloud Connector simplifies managing your cloud-to-on-premise connections. It allows administrators to handle access controls, authentication, and certificate management in one place. Consequently, you ensure that only authorized SAP cloud services can connect to your internal systems, maintaining consistency and simplifying governance.

Granular and Precise Access Control
Even if OData links seem publicly reachable, the Cloud Connector gives you detailed control over exactly which services, APIs, or datasets external applications can access. This granular management greatly reduces the risk of unauthorized data exposure, ensuring compliance and protecting sensitive information. Facilitating SAP BTP Services (SAP Build Apps, Integration Suite, and More)

Beyond security, the Cloud Connector is vital for enabling seamless and effective integration of SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) services, such as SAP Build Apps, SAP Integration Suite, and SAP Analytics Cloud. It ensures these cloud-based applications can reliably and securely interact with your on-premise SAP backend systems, enhancing their functionality and simplifying integration tasks.

In summary, SAP Cloud Connector doesn't just enhance security—it simplifies integration, provides centralized control, and significantly enhances the effectiveness and security of your SAP cloud integrations

Service Creation and Interface Configuration

This blog primarily covers setting up the Cloud Connector. If you're new to SAP, additional background information about available data and deployment strategies is available.

SAP recommends using predefined CDS views to securely expose data via OData services. CDS views offer control over selecting relevant fields, enable data transformations, and help enforce security and business rules through built-in annotations and validations.

You can create OData services rapidly through automatic service generation or develop more complex services manually using the SAP Gateway Service Builder (SEGW). Once created, services must be activated using transaction /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE and can be tested with /IWFND/GW_CLIENT. This link provides more information: https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-and-automation-blog-posts/a-step-by-step-proces...

 

Setting Up the Cloud Connector

Establishing a secure interface using the Cloud Connector involves several key steps:

1. Installation:

  1. Download: Begin by downloading the latest version of the Cloud Connector: https://tools.hana.ondemand.com/#cloud 
  2. Install: Before you install the Cloud Connector make sure that the newest Java 8 version is installed: https://www.oracle.com/nl/java/technologies/javase/javase8-archive-downloads.html.To install the cloud connector it is possible to click true all the steps in the setup
  3. Open: To open the Cloud Connector run the program “Start Cloud Connector” as administrator and enter this link in your web browser: https://localhost:8443 and click on proceed to local host.

To login use the Username: Administrator and the Password: manage. In the next page you can set your new password and proceed with the initial setup. for the Initial setup choose Master (primary installation) and press save. 

2. Configuring Subaccount:

To connect to the SAP Subaccount, press the button + Add Sub Account. The following pop up will be displayed:

Connecting to the internet through an HTTPS proxy, as prompted here in SAP Cloud Connector Administration, may be necessary in the following scenarios:

When to Use an HTTPS Proxy

  1. Cloud Integration Needs: If your SAP environment connects to external cloud services like SAP BTP Integration Suite, an HTTPS proxy ensures secure, reliable data tunnels between on-premises systems and the cloud. Even if the Cloud Connector is hosted locally, connecting to SAP BTP (a cloud service) typically requires a proxy unless your network allows direct, secure internet access.
  2. Enhanced Security & Compliance: Proxies add a layer of protection by monitoring traffic, helping meet regulatory standards and internal security policies.
  3. Restricted Corporate Networks: If your network blocks direct internet access, a proxy is mandatory to enable external communication while adhering to company policies.
  4. Simplified Maintenance: Proxies streamline software updates and patches, keeping your Cloud Connector secure and up-to-date.

When to Skip the Proxy

  1. Secure Direct Internet Access: If your network already allows secure direct connections to SAP BTP endpoints (e.g., *.hana.ondemand.com), a proxy may add unnecessary complexity.
  2. Internal Use Only: If the Cloud Connector doesn’t interact with external services, a proxy offers no benefit and can be omitted.
  3. Prioritizing Simplicity: Proxies introduce configuration overhead. If your setup values minimalism, bypassing the proxy reduces management effort.

If you need to configure an HTTPS proxy for SAP Cloud Connector, here's what you typically fill in: But for this example we leave the proxy empty. 

Host: Enter the proxy server’s hostname or IP address provided by your organization's network team (Example: proxy.mycompany.com or 192.168.1.1)

Port: The proxy server's port number, often 8080, 443, or another port specified by your IT department.

User: Proxy username, if authentication is required.

(Leave empty if no authentication is necessary.)

Password: Corresponding password for the proxy user.

(Leave empty if no authentication is necessary.)

After this step, open your BTP environment, here you can find the relevant data you need to connect SAP BTW with your cloud connector. You can choose to add all the data manually, or go to Cloud Connectors and download Authentication data. 

After downloading this data go back to your cloud connector and select configure using authentication data and choose, add sub account authentication data from file and select the file that you just downloaded. 

You can now Check if there is a secure connection in the BTP platform and in your cloud connector. This should look somewhat like this:

!If the Cloud Connector cannot establish a connection to your BTP account, first verify that your Windows network profile is set to “Private.” At home the Connector often works on “Public” because your router allows port 8443 and outbound HTTPS by default, but in a corporate setting a firewall or proxy will block these connections. Switching to “Private,” configuring the Connector with your company’s proxy, or asking IT to open ports 8443 and 443 to *.hana.ondemand.com will restore connectivity.

3. Configuring Certifications:

When setting up HTTPS communication between the SAP Cloud Connector and your SAP backend system, properly managing certificates in the SAP Trust Manager (transaction STRUST) is essential. Certificates act like digital passports—they verify identities, establish trust between systems, and ensure that data is encrypted during transmission. But how does this actually work in SAP? And where exactly do these certificates go? Let’s break it down.

Identity Certificates vs. Trust Anchors in STRUST

In each Personal Security Environment  in STRUST, there are two types of certificates to be aware of:

1. Identity Certificate ("Own Certificate")

This is your SAP system’s own server certificate—it contains a private key and is used when SAP needs to prove its identity (e.g., during TLS handshakes). Only one identity certificate can be active per PSE at any given time.

!If you replace this certificate, you must update all other devices to trust the new one; otherwise, connections may fail!

2. Trusted Certificates ("Certificate List")

These are public certificates your SAP system uses to trust other systems—such as the Cloud Connector or its CA. These are also called trust anchors. You can store multiple trusted certificates here without issue.

Regularly reviewing this list helps avoid trust errors or certificate expiration surprises.

Where to Place Certificates in STRUST

When your SAP backend receives inbound HTTPS connections (such as requests from the SAP Cloud Connector), it uses a specific trust store in STRUST called the SSL System-wide Standard PSE.

To establish trust, you typically need to upload the Cloud Connector’s certificate or the CA certificate that signed it into this PSE. This tells your SAP system: “This incoming connection is safe and trusted.”

Important: You do not need to upload this certificate if it's already signed by a widely trusted root CA that your SAP system recognizes by default. In many cases, SAP already includes a list of common root CAs—so the Cloud Connector will be trusted automatically.

Configuring Certificates

When setting up HTTPS communication between the SAP Cloud Connector and your SAP backend system, properly managing certificates in the SAP Trust Manager (transaction STRUST) is essential. The goal is to ensure that your SAP system both presents a valid identity certificate when required and trusts the relevant external certificates to establish a secure SSL/TLS handshake.

Identity Certificates vs. Trust Anchors in STRUST

In each Personal Security Environment (PSE) in STRUST, there are two types of certificates to be aware of:

When dealing with certificates in STRUST, it’s important to understand the distinction between identity certificates (also called “Own Certificates”) and trust anchors (trusted certificates in the Certificate List). These serve different purposes:

  • An identity certificate is the certificate that identifies your SAP system. It usually includes a private key and is used by SAP when acting as a server or client to present its identity to others. For example, if your SAP system is serving HTTPS requests, it will present its identity certificate to the client (browser or Cloud Connector) during the SSL handshake. In STRUST, the identity certificate is shown in the “Own Certificate” section of a PSE.

  • Trust anchors are the certificates of external authorities or systems that your SAP system trusts. In other words, these are usually root CA certificates or intermediate certificates that form the chain of trust for the certificates presented by others. They do not contain a private key in your system; they are public certificates that you add to SAP so that it recognizes and trusts the identities of external parties. In STRUST, trusted certificates are listed in the “Certificate List” section of a PSE (the lower pane). For example, if you need to call an external web service over HTTPS, you must import the web service’s root CA certificate as a trust anchor. This way, when SAP (as a client) connects, it can verify the server’s certificate against these trusted CAs.

Understanding the Difference Between PSE Types

Finally, it’s important to clearly understand the two primary Personal Security Environment Types we’ve discussed and when each is used:

  • SSL System-wide Standard PSE (SSL Server PSE): This PSE is used when the SAP system acts as a server in an SSL/TLS communication. In other words, for inbound connections to SAP. The System-wide SSL Server PSE provides the server’s identity certificate and uses its trust list to verify clients if needed. “System-wide” means one certificate can be used across all application servers in the system​ (as opposed to having a separate certificate per instance).

  • SSL Client (Standard) PSE: This PSE is used when the SAP system acts as a client making outbound HTTPS requests. For any outbound connection that SAP initiates (calling a REST API, connecting to an external cloud service, etc.), the SSL Client (Standard) PSE is consulted. It contains the list of trusted CA certificates that SAP will accept for server certificates on the other side.

  • Be careful where you import certificates: Always choose the correct PSE based on the direction of the connection. Importing a certificate into the wrong PSE, like putting a client certificate into the system-wide PSE, can disrupt communication with other systems and cause unexpected connection failures.

Creating Certificates:

If your SAP system doesn’t already have a background trust certificate (X.509), you can generate one using one of the methods outlined below. However, it's rare for this certificate to be missing, so if you're unsure, it's best to consult your network specialist.

  • Using XCA (recommended due to its user-friendly GUI): https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/how-to-guide-xca-quick-start-guide/ba-p/133254... 
  • Using OpenSSL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z2l-5utm3WY 

Exporting Existing Certificates:

To export an existing certificate from SAP, go to transaction STRUST in the PSE SSL Server Standard system wide and double click on one of the issuer certificates: 

Now the certificate is opened on the bottom of the page. Press the button export certificate.

A new tab is opened where you can select the location, name of the certificate and the file format. After downloading your certificate go to the cloud connector site and select configuration. On the configuration screen select on premise.

Go to the tab Backend Trust Store allowlist and press the button add x509 certificate.

Upload your certificate here. It is not necessary to upload a CA Certificate because the system already trusts publicly signed certificates from standard Certificate Authorities (CAs).

4. Configuring Connection Paths:

After making sure the right certificates are in place we can continue with connecting the system. Open the sap cloud connector site and select Cloud to On premise in the left menu. and press the button “add system mapping”. For the back end type are several options available. For this case we choose SAP HANA, but it is also possible to select other back end systems.

 

Field

Entry

Back-end Type

SAP HANA: Select this option to specify that the backend system is an SAP HANA database.

Protocol

HTTPS: Choose HTTPS to ensure that communication with the backend system is encrypted and secure.

Internal Host

Enter the Fully Qualified Domain Name (FQDN) or hostname of the machine where the Production Connector is installed. A mismatch between the entered hostname and the certificate will result in SSL errors and block the connection. I used the command /IWFND/GW_CLIENT or /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE to inspect the request url. For example https://prod-connector.company.com:44321/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZC_142JJ_MATERIALSPERUSER_CDS/?$format=xm.... Use the red text as the internal host. 

Internal Port

Enter the port number used by the Production Connector for cloud communication (e.g., 443 or a custom HTTPS port).  For example https://prod-connector.company.com:44321/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZC_142JJ_MATERIALSPERUSER_CDS/?$format=xm.... Use the red text as the internal host. 

Virtual Host

Enter a unique virtual host name, such as prodconccms, to identify this Production Connector instance. Only use letters (A–Z, a–z), numbers (0–9), and periods (.). Special characters are not allowed.

Virtual Port

Enter a virtual port number, for example: 50066.This will be used together with the virtual host to form the URL, e.g., https://prodconccms:50066. The virtual port can be the same as the internal port if desired.

Principal Propagation

Deselect this option.Certificate-based OAuth is now used for authentication instead of principal propagation.

System Certificate for Logon

Select this checkbox to enable certificate-based authentication for logging into the system.

Host In Request Header

Use the default setting: Use Virtual HostThis ensures the virtual host name is sent in the request headers.

Description

No input required.This field can be left empty unless a description is specifically needed.

Check Internal Host

Select this checkbox to verify that the internal host name is reachable and correctly configured.

The result should look like this and the status should be reachable:

Define “Cloud to On‑Premise” Paths: Configuring resource path mappings is what actually grants your cloud applications access to specific services in your internal network. Without this step, even though the Cloud Connector is installed and connected to your subaccount, no data can flow. These mappings define exactly which internal systems and paths the Cloud Connector is allowed to expose to the cloud, acting as a secure filter to prevent unauthorized access.

When a request comes from SAP BTP, such as a call to an OData service, it passes through the Cloud Connector. Here’s where resource path mappings come in: they translate the virtual host and path requested by the cloud app into the actual internal system address and path, ensuring that the request reaches the right backend component (e.g., SAP Gateway, ABAP system, or ERP backend).

 I used the command /IWFND/GW_CLIENT or/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE to inspect the request url. For example https://prod-connector.company.com:44321/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZC_142JJ_MATERIALSPERUSER_CDS/?$format=xm.... Use the red text as the path. Additionally, set the Access Policy to “Path and all Sub-Paths” to ensure the system allows access to the specified path as well as any nested resources and query options beneath it.

5. Create Destination:

Go to the connectivity tab in SAP BTP and check if your cloud connector has an exposed back end system. And that the resources are available.

The next step is to create a new Destination. For this go to SAP BTP Connectivity and then Destinations. And then press the button to create a new Destination. Use your virtual host as url like this example below. Set proxy type to OnPremise and use basic authentication and use your own SAP username and password. Also don't forget to use the Additional Properties mentioned in the example below and set your SAP-Client to your own client. 

6. Test your Connection 

When you go to the SAP application studio select the Data source Connect to a System. You can select your own system (Destination that we just created). Here are all services that are opened with the URL resource path in the SAP CLOUD connector.

Final Thoughts

Installing the Cloud Connector is a straightforward process, but the many available options can make it challenging for beginners to set up a connection. I wrote this blog during my SAP traineeship, so I'm not an expert in certificates or network configurations. If you spot any mistakes or have suggestions for improvement, I'd really appreciate your feedback!

entry.title: QM Inspection lot with physical samples final result.
entry.content:
Hi there,

When creating an Inspection lot with physical samples, which are generated automatically, I am able to enter value result for any sample, then the final result is the average of all these values.

entry.title: Re: List Report V4 - Hide Copy action button
entry.content: in manisfest we can set "disableCopyToClipboard": true, it'll resolve the issue.

entry.title: Re: Can the flavor builder role be limited in scope?
entry.content:
It is possible to restrict scripts creation for builder. For that similar to /PERSONAS/EDITOR_ROLE role must be assigned for Flavor builder but:

  • remove "06 - Macros" activity from P_EDIT Authorisation Object - This will remove possibility to assign scripts from Flavor Manager
  • remove "05 - Scripting" activity from P_RUNTIME Authorisation Object - This will remove possibility to start Script Editor

with such modifications, it will be impossible to create and then use scripts.

entry.title: Re: CDS Table Entities - for On-Prem & Private Cloud
entry.content: And yes, the CDS table entities have been available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition, since release 2502. See here:  https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a098626518824cfe80907fd6f51b069b/ddbdbaafcbc54e26b23ca566698240ab.

entry.title: Re: Attributes-to-Sap for Excel
entry.content:
Hello Gerhard,

I am using ECTR version 1.1.7.4.

This resource unfortunately does not meet my needs.

For example, I need a resource like the one below. There are Customizing.pdfs for Catia or Solidworks.

But I could not find such a resource for Excel.

Regards

Nazif Kara

 

4.3 Mapping Settings in the SAP Engineering Control Center Interface to

CATIA V5

Similar to the settings for mapping data from SAP Engineering Control Center to the

integration SAP Engineering Control Center to CATIA V5, the settings for mapping from CATIA

V5 into SAP can be found in the file attributes-to-sap.xml. In the example below you can see

how the data mapping needs to be defined. Any feature within the xml syntax, apart from

special keywords for the CATIA integration which are described here, can be found in the

operations guide of ECTR.

In the example below you can see, how CATIA values are mapped to SAP, e.g.

Classification:

<section name="CAT_PR" description="Product mapping from CATIA to ←-

,→ SAP">

<DIR field="Description">

<APPL_ATTRIBUTE name="S-Description "/>

</DIR>

<DIR_CLS class="Z_CENIT" classtype="017" characteristic="←-

,→ KUNDENNAME">

<APPL_ATTRIBUTE name="P-Kundenname"/>

</DIR_CLS>

<DIR_CLS class="Z_CENIT" classtype="017" characteristic="←-

,→ WindowsUser">

<APPL_ATTRIBUTE name="E-Username"/>

</DIR_CLS>

<DIR_CLS class="Z_CENIT" classtype="017" characteristic="←-

,→ PartDefault">

<APPL_ATTRIBUTE name="U-Standard"/>

</DIR_CLS>

</section>

entry.title: Reasons for Rejection is error in the report
entry.content:
Hi,

 

1.  In APP IMG – sales-basic functions-Define Reasons for rejection , I add the Rejection reason ZX which statistics is X , and add the Rejection reason ZY which statistics is Y.

 

2. In APP Manage sales orders , I create sales order 582 . The order quantity of item 1 is 10 PCS , the net value is 1228.5

In APP Create outbound delivery with order reference , I post the outbound delivery 80000465 . The quantity of item 1 is 5 PCS .

In APP Manage sales orders , I change the sales order 582 , and set Rejection reason ZX to item 1.

But In the CDS I_SalesDocumentItem , I find that the qty is 10 , it should be 5.

And In the CDS I_SalesOrderItemCube , I find that the qty is 10 , it should be 5.

 

3.In APP Manage sales orders , I create sales order 583 . The order quantity of item 1 is 10 PCS , the net value is 1228.5

In APP Create outbound delivery with order reference , I post the outbound delivery 80000466 . The quantity of item 1 is 5 PCS .

In APP Manage sales orders , I change the sales order 583 , and set Rejection reason ZY to item 1.

But In the CDS I_SalesDocumentItem , I find that the qty is 10 , it should be 5.

And In the CDS I_SalesOrderItemCube , I find that the qty is 10 , it should be 5.

4. I find that the  statistics  of  Rejection reason , X and Y , the report can't show the correct the qty and net value.

There is nothing you can do yourself except open a case with SAP HANA Cloud Support in SAP for Me (if the problem persists).

entry.title: Re: CORUPROC transaction not clearing AFFW data
entry.content: CORUPROC is a re-processing tool. It will not delete AFFW records and will not resolve your shortdumps. You need to address the root cause, which means you should investigate the MESSAGE_TYPE_X dumps. Where does the error occur? What is the error message in the dump (e.g. CO 888 or RU 803)? With these findings you can then take it further and check on OSS.

entry.content:
When we (The Customer side Basis Team)  receive an SAP System from SAP ECS RISE Basis Team, we need to check the system and make sure that it is set up as intended and contains everything that was expected, let's call it a post delivery inspection.

This comment will be the placeholder for all of the "Basis" checks we do on a SAP System delivered by SAP ECS RISE Basis Team as the Post Delivery Inspection before we do the "Post Installation". ["Post Installation" will be in the next comment]....

The Post Delivery Inspection steps will begin in an agnostic foundation way enabling checking of any SAP System, S/4, S/4 Foundation, and subsequently will consider checks for systems like GRC (AC 12 on S/4HANA), GTS, aATP, EWM, TM, MDG, etc....

.

entry.title: S4 Readiness Checker report out of BW7.5 System - Forbidden Object Found - Error messages ..
entry.content:
We are just about to start our S4 journey and are in the initial phase of preparatory checks and readiness stages.

One of the thing that was carried out was running a S4 Readiness check for our BW system which has thrown up a myriad of errors in various areas of BW such as Update rules, transformations, process chains, DTPs to name a few.

A very common theme behind the vast majority of error is the use of objects that were allowed in BW 7.5 but  are forbidden in the world of S4.

A few examples of these errors are ( there are a few 100s of these types of forbidden errors):

  • Code FUNC: Found match(es) of forbidden object FUNC CONVERT_TO_STAT_CURRENCY.
  • Code PROG: Found match(es) of forbidden object PROG RSBCTGLS_INCL_GLOBAL_SPEND.
  • Code TABL: Found match(es) of forbidden object TABL GLS_CNT_REDEL.
  • Code DTEL: Found match(es) of forbidden object DTEL SR_VE_GNLOCATIO.

In spite of numerous searches online, I am unable to figure out the fixes to these type of errors should be. If an  BW object is forbidden in S4, then is there a replacement object for it in S4 ? Is there a composite OSS note  that gives a list of possible replacements by code type ? What approach should be take to resolve these errors. Our first step at getting ready for S4 to convert every single 3.x Update rule to 7.x transformation.  But before we do that I would like to know how we are supposed to handle these forbidden objects errors. 

Am sure we are not the first ones to have encountered this. So please if anyone could give me some sort of steer in the right direction to tackle these messages , that would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Treating Business Goals as "Individual" Goals possible?
entry.content:
@xavierlegarrec  maybe I was wrong or explaining little bit wrong. Its not really a weightage of goals or goal sections, its more the weightage of payout. I will give an example:

 

For easier comparison I used 100k as salary: Salary 100.000€, Target percentage 10% -> Target Amount 10.000€

These 10.000€ are now split into two parts: a) Billable Hours and b) Goal Achievement (in total). These two parts (a) and (b) can now differ in weightage of their payout. It can be (a)= 50% & (B)= 50% of target (5000 & 5000) or maybe (a)= 70% (b)=30% (7000 & 3000). This split is determined at the beginning of each year and differs from employee to employee.  

There are no additional (or corporate) goals to be considered. 

What I was thinking of was something like having the billable hours part as custom fields (of course theres some calculation for overfulfilment and so on) and the goals from PMGM as indivudual goals. I calculate the 10.000 into two parts according to their weightage (i.e. 70/30) and multiply each part it with its achievement percentage from billable or goals and add them togehter for payout.

Quick remark: Billable hours, billable achievement and "payout split" -> available in CompInfo

Is something like this feasible?

Thank you!

Michael

entry.title: Re: Deliv.Rel. Billg Sts field in SO
entry.content:
Hello @KhaingZinTun 

For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the field you want is “Deliv.Rel. Billg Sts”—is available via the released CDS view:

 I_SalesOrderItem

    Publicly released CDS view accessible via the View Browser or Custom CDS Views app.

    Contains the field BillingStatusCode, which corresponds to the “Delivery‑Related Billing Status” you’re seeing in Fiori

Steps to Validate

  •     Open View Browser in your Public Cloud tenant.
  •     Search for and preview I_SalesOrderItem.

    Verify that BillingStatusCode is present. It holds values like

  •         A = Not invoiced
  •         B = Partially invoiced
  •         C = Fully invoiced

These status values align with what Fiori displays as “Deliv.Rel. Billg Sts”    .

 Summary Table

Requirement

Public Cloud Solution

Field “Deliv.Rel. Billg Sts”

BillingStatusCode in I_SalesOrderItem

Access tools

View Browser or Custom CDS Views app

Valid values

A / B / C (Not / Partially / Fully invoiced)

 Next Steps

  •     Open View Browser → I_SalesOrderItem → verify BillingStatusCode.
  •     You can optionally create a Custom CDS View, select this field, and publish it for use in Fiori reports or analytic tiles.

SAP help reference:

CDS Views for Sales

Thank you and kind regards

Chuma

entry.title: Common issues in SAP Joule Activation in SAP S/4 HANA Private Cloud Edition
entry.content:
Common Issues in SAP Joule activation for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

 This blog is continuation of our comprehensive setup blog. In last several months, we helped multiple customers in activating Joule for their S/4 HANA Private Cloud systems  and in this blog we will compile all the common issues we faced and it's potential fix and workaround. 

  1. Issue: Joule refused to connect after the launch from S/4 HANA Fiori launchpad.

 

Reason: Trusted domain configuration not done.

Fix:  Add the Fiori launchpad URL (Host details) in both IAS-> Tenant configuration and BTP Trust settings. Please note that the trusted domain might take some time to take effect. Please retry launching Joule after 15-20 minutes in private browsing window. 

  

Screenshots

    

 

 

  1. Issue: Error occurred “Digital Assistant not found”.

Reason: S/4 HANA system is not added in the global account system formation.

Fix: Delete the existing formation for joule in BTP global account. In case of any error in formation creation, create a ticket with the component BC-CP-MP.

Screenshots

 

 

  1. Issue: Content provider synchronization fails with unauthorized error.

Reason:

  1. The user credential is invalid.
  2. The user is not having enough roles.
  3. Cloud connector version is > 2.15 and principal type “None” or “Use system certificate checkbox is checked”

Fix:

  1. Check and ensure the user credential is correct in DT destination.
  2. Ensure the user has SAP_FLP_ADMIN and SAP_FLP_EXP_USER roles.
  3. In the cloud connector make sure that the principal type is selected as “X.509 certificate”

Screenshots

 

 

 

 

 

 

Solution screenshot in cloud connector:

 

 

  1. Issue: Editing in IPS transformation, adding additional parameter failed with unspecified error.

Reason: Source / Target transformation contains error.

Fix: Copy the latest transformation from https://help.sap.com/docs/JOULE/6189c8655c484916bb8eb767126a653a/a6cf317ecede4fa78c57be07219cee1a.html?version=CLOUD

 

Screenshots

 

 

  1. Issue: Transactional query fetches informational / navigational result.

Reason: It can be due to many issues which may include the following.

  1. User doesn’t have the required roles for the transactional queries.
  2. OData service required by the transactional scenario is not active.
  3. Check the content provider configuration.
  4. Issue with the principal propagation setup.

Fix:

  1. Check the business roles required by the given transactional queries from the SAP Note: https://me.sap.com/notes/3523238 and ensure the user has the required roles. In case using the custom roles, ensure the required semantic objects and action are available in the custom roles.
  2. Check the status of the OData services as given in the above note and ensure they are active.
  3. Additionally, you can check the runtime destination for dynamic data in the content provider has the PCE SSO destination.
  4. Verify the principal propagation setup and check the cloud connector log, ICM log to perform troubleshooting.

 

Screenshots

The below screenshot depicts the resolution 3.

 

  1. Issue: Joule icon is not showing up in S/4 HANA Fiori launchpad after all the configurations.

Reason:

  1. User is not having the role which contains the business catalogue for Joule plug-in.
  2. Cache issue.
  3. 3rd party cookie is blocked by the browser setting.
  4. Fiori customizing is causing conflict with configuration.

 

Fix:

  1. Ensure that the user has the “Single Role” which contains the Fiori business catalogue.
  2. Clear the browser cache. Also, we can try invalidating the server cache by executing the program: /UI2/INVALIDATE_GLOBAL_CACHES
  3. Allow the 3rd party cookie in the browser setting.
  4. Use T-code: /UI2/FLC to check the launchpad content first with the setting “Configuration” and if no error appeared then run it for “Customizing”.

Check the following SAP Help documentation for more information:

 https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_FIORI_OVERVIEW/47d8a2e60bf2442c8e0675961953eef6/21484a647f9f4563942da3e846b5cc33.html

 

Screenshots

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Issue: launching joule ends up in error “Corp IDP” refused to connect.

Reason: It can happen due to multiple reasons. The most common we have seen is, the S/4 HANA Fiori launchpad is directly connected to corporate IDP (Microsoft Azure in this example) without having SAP Cloud Identity service as proxy.

Fix: It’s recommended by SAP to always use the same Cloud identity services tenant in Fiori launchpad which has been used with the BTP for Joule.

Screenshots

 

 

  1. Issue: Error in adding the Build work zone and IAS in formation after running Joule booster.

Reason:  The Identity authentication is not enabled in SAP Build work zone.

Fix: Enable the Identity authentication in SAP Build work zone.

Screenshots

 

 

  1. Issue: Cloud connector is not able to connect subaccount with Cloud connector

     

 

Fix: Download the authentication data from sub account and upload it in cloud connector, it’ll connect.

Thanks to my colleagues in @Diganta_Mandal @nageshcaparthy and others in AI RIG, AI Factory and Product teams. 

We will update this blog as we face more common setup issues.

Hi Rehan,

Great showcase of possibilities!

I tried to create scenario with Business Partner on S4H on prem 2023. Derived event works in terms of payload extended, but filter is not showing in outbound binding. 

i tried many things:

- delete topic, add topic

- clear cache of channel in /IWXBE/R_CACHE_CLEANUP

- do some changes in data view, BDEF extension, Event binding and activate again

- add @Metadata.allowExtensions: true , but this is rather needed only for standard data view

here is my CDS:

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED

@EndUserText.label: 'Data definition for BP'

@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

@ObjectModel.usageType:{

serviceQuality: #X,

sizeCategory: #S,

dataClass: #MIXED

}

 

define view entity ZMP_BusinessPartner_DD2 as select from I_BusinessPartner

{

key BusinessPartner,

@event.context.attribute: 'xsapfirstname'

FirstName,

LastName,

SearchTerm1,

BusinessPartnerCategory,

BusinessPartnerUUID

} where FirstName = 'Bernd 3'

entry.title: Re: limitations of SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) when it come...
entry.content:
Hi Ujjani

I am not sure I can answer in detail to your questions, but here are a few elements:

1) & 2) There are different levels where you can carry out calculations in SAP analytics cloud:

  • The modeler
  • The story (a.k.a. report in WebI) at the object level
  • The table front end (predefined aggregations)

You can check a list of available ones.

3) Yes, you can schedule publications for an output in .pdf .csv & .pptx. You can check the possibilities here. It is not yet as powerful as BOE scheduling.

Regarding the treillis, you can leverage linked analysis feature to dynamically filter/explore various visualizations.

Hope this helps!

Greg

entry.title: Re: Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - A Comprehensive Setup Guide
entry.content:
Hi @beatebea ,

You can't log into Joule with your S/4 HANA user name and Password. However, you can definitely have seamless Joule experience without having to double log on, if you have SSO setup between your S/4 HANA and Cloud Identity services. 

You must use the same CIS tenant for S/4 and BTP. 

 

Best regards,

Samir

entry.title: Re: RFOPENPOSTING_FR functionality
entry.content:
Hi @prasadgarude90 

RFOPENPOSTING_FR is basically a legal requirement in France for tax audit reporting.

It can be used for many other balance sheet accounts and not just Customer and Vendor reconciliation accounts. but this is used only for Open item management balance sheet accounts.

Its mostly used for reconciliation accounts because they require open item tracking but it can be used for other balance sheet open item accounts which are require open item tracking. 

I hope this answers your query.

 

 

Regards

Vijaykumar

entry.title: Re: Cloud ALM - Business Service Management strategy
entry.content:
Hello Raja,
The Business Service Management will help you to group your systems (and cloud services) into business services which can be understand by the business users.
It provides the possibility to offer a different view apart from the technical landscape definition (like a 4-tier landscape) - to represent business functionality rather then transport routes for example. 
You can find more information on our SAP Cloud ALM Expert Portal for Business Service Management here.
Let me know if this answers your question. 
best regards,
Romy
SAP Support

entry.title: Error with transporting assignment of BP Identification Category to Identification Type
entry.content:
Dear Community,

in our Public Cloud Implementation Project, we would like to assign a customized Business Partner Identification Category to a BP Identification Type.

Identification Categories have been customized. Transport from customizing to test system in public cloud has been done via Export Software Collection. By releasing the software collection in its specific app, the transport is immediately available in the test system and ready to be imported.

Afterwards, we assign the Identification Category to an Identification Type. This setting has to be stored in a customizing transport.  Subsequently, the customizing transport has to be released in order to transport it to the test system. This is not working due to dependencies to the settings, that had to be stored in the software collection.

 

Does anyone of you know how to overcome the issue? A Ticket has already been raised.

 

Many thanks in advance and kind regards

Talke

entry.title: Re: SAP -Generative AI
entry.content:
HI @MohanMujumale - Transparency is key when it comes to AI,  I have therefore added the "GenAI Assisted Content" user tag to your post, according to our Rules of Engagement: "If you want to publish content that was helped/created by GenAI, you must add the user tag GenAI Assisted Content. For content where it's not possible to add a user tag (e.g., answers), you must include this statement: "GenAI was used to help generate this content."

The post in its current form is also too short. 
We expect every blog post to consist of a beginning (introduction), a middle (main portion), and an end (a conclusion that wraps everything up). A collection of links or just a video with short text does not qualify as a blog post. You can find more information about writing blog posts in our FAQ.

entry.title: Re: Employment Status in Story reports
entry.content: Hi Punita, Grey "Status" in Basic User Information looks like 100% permission issue. You can check it, if you switch on "Unconditional access to the data" in the Reporting section in your role and then have access to that field in the report, that would mean that you lacked some permission for sure. Second option is to use "Employment status" dimension from Job information for filtering where you need to exclude "Terminated"
